title: Assignment problem variation
description: Solve assignment using min cost flow

examples.explanation: n=2. Start backtrack(0): worker=0, try task=0: used[0]=True, assignment[0]=0. backtrack(1): worker=1, try task=1: used[1]=True, assignment[1]=1. backtrack(2): worker=2==n, calculate cost: cost_matrix[0][0]+cost_matrix[1][1]=1+4=5, min_cost=5. Backtrack: used[1]=False, assignment[1]=-1. worker=1 try task=0: but used[0]=True, skip. Return to worker=0: used[0]=False, assignment[0]=-1. worker=0 try task=1: used[1]=True, assignment[0]=1. backtrack(1): worker=1, try task=0: used[0]=True, assignment[1]=0. backtrack(2): calculate cost: cost_matrix[0][1]+cost_matrix[1][0]=2+3=5, min_cost remains 5. Final min_cost=5.

n=3. We'll trace key steps: Generate permutations: [0,1,2]: cost=5+4+9=18; [0,2,1]: 5+6+8=19; [1,0,2]: 3+2+9=14; [1,2,0]: 3+6+7=16; [2,0,1]: 1+2+8=11; [2,1,0]: 1+4+7=12. Actually let's do systematic backtrack: Start assignment=[-1,-1,-1], used=[F,F,F]. backtrack(0): try task=0: assign, backtrack(1): try task=1: assign, backtrack(2): try task=2: assign, cost=5+4+9=18, min=18. Backtrack, try task=2: assign, cost=5+6+8=19, min=18. Backtrack to worker=0 try task=1: assign, backtrack(1): try task=0: assign, cost=3+2+9=14, min=14; try task=2: assign, cost=3+6+7=16, min=14. Backtrack to worker=0 try task=2: assign, backtrack(1): try task=0: assign, cost=1+2+8=11, min=11; try task=1: assign, cost=1+4+7=12, min=11. Final min=11. Wait, recalc: [2,0,1] means worker0 gets task2 cost=1, worker1 gets task0 cost=2, worker2 gets task1 cost=8, total=11. Check others: [2,1,0]=1+4+7=12. So min=11. But earlier I said 10, let's verify matrix: row0:[5,3,1], row1:[2,4,6], row2:[7,8,9]. Assignment [2,0,1]=1+2+8=11. [2,1,0]=1+4+7=12. [0,1,2]=5+4+9=18. [0,2,1]=5+6+8=19. [1,0,2]=3+2+9=14. [1,2,0]=3+6+7=16. Yes min=11. I'll correct output to 11.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(n!)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible assignments of tasks to workers by exploring all permutations, calculating the total cost for each assignment, and keeping track of the minimum. This approach is conceptually simple but only practical for very small problem sizes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Assignment Problem
ladders.desc: Find the minimum cost assignment of n tasks to n workers by exploring all permutations.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force backtracking solution to the assignment problem. The intuition is that we need to try every possible way to assign tasks to workers, since each worker must do exactly one task and each task must be assigned to exactly one worker. This corresponds to finding all permutations of task assignments. We'll use backtracking to systematically generate all permutations, calculating the total cost for each complete assignment and keeping track of the minimum. The algorithm starts with an empty assignment and recursively builds it by trying each unassigned task for the current worker. When we reach a complete assignment (all workers have tasks), we calculate the total cost from the cost matrix and update our minimum if needed. Time complexity is O(n!) because we generate all n! permutations. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and current assignment array. This connects to L1 by using the permutation generation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not properly backtracking (forgetting to unassign tasks), not handling the base case correctly, or inefficiently calculating costs. Real code pattern: def min_cost_assignment(cost_matrix): n = len(cost_matrix); min_cost = float('inf'); assignment = [-1]*n; used = [False]*n; def backtrack(worker): if worker == n: calculate cost; update min; return; for task in range(n): if not used[task]: used[task]=True; assignment[worker]=task; backtrack(worker+1); used[task]=False; assignment[worker]=-1; backtrack(0); return min_cost.

examples.trace: n=2. Start backtrack(0): worker=0, try task=0: used[0]=True, assignment[0]=0. backtrack(1): worker=1, try task=1: used[1]=True, assignment[1]=1. backtrack(2): worker=2==n, calculate cost: cost_matrix[0][0]+cost_matrix[1][1]=1+4=5, min_cost=5. Backtrack: used[1]=False, assignment[1]=-1. worker=1 try task=0: but used[0]=True, skip. Return to worker=0: used[0]=False, assignment[0]=-1. worker=0 try task=1: used[1]=True, assignment[0]=1. backtrack(1): worker=1, try task=0: used[0]=True, assignment[1]=0. backtrack(2): calculate cost: cost_matrix[0][1]+cost_matrix[1][0]=2+3=5, min_cost remains 5. Final min_cost=5.

n=3. We'll trace key steps: Generate permutations: [0,1,2]: cost=5+4+9=18; [0,2,1]: 5+6+8=19; [1,0,2]: 3+2+9=14; [1,2,0]: 3+6+7=16; [2,0,1]: 1+2+8=11; [2,1,0]: 1+4+7=12. Actually let's do systematic backtrack: Start assignment=[-1,-1,-1], used=[F,F,F]. backtrack(0): try task=0: assign, backtrack(1): try task=1: assign, backtrack(2): try task=2: assign, cost=5+4+9=18, min=18. Backtrack, try task=2: assign, cost=5+6+8=19, min=18. Backtrack to worker=0 try task=1: assign, backtrack(1): try task=0: assign, cost=3+2+9=14, min=14; try task=2: assign, cost=3+6+7=16, min=14. Backtrack to worker=0 try task=2: assign, backtrack(1): try task=0: assign, cost=1+2+8=11, min=11; try task=1: assign, cost=1+4+7=12, min=11. Final min=11. Wait, recalc: [2,0,1] means worker0 gets task2 cost=1, worker1 gets task0 cost=2, worker2 gets task1 cost=8, total=11. Check others: [2,1,0]=1+4+7=12. So min=11. But earlier I said 10, let's verify matrix: row0:[5,3,1], row1:[2,4,6], row2:[7,8,9]. Assignment [2,0,1]=1+2+8=11. [2,1,0]=1+4+7=12. [0,1,2]=5+4+9=18. [0,2,1]=5+6+8=19. [1,0,2]=3+2+9=14. [1,2,0]=3+6+7=16. Yes min=11. I'll correct output to 11.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Permutations via Backtracking
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of n elements using backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This is the core subroutine for the brute force approach: generating all possible assignments by creating permutations of tasks. The intuition is that each permutation represents one complete assignment where the i-th element is the task assigned to worker i. We use backtracking to build permutations recursively: start with an empty list, at each position try all elements that haven't been used yet, add one, recurse to the next position, then backtrack by removing it. The algorithm: function generate_permutations(n): result = []; current = []; used = [False]*n; def backtrack(): if len(current)==n: result.append(current.copy()); return; for i in range(n): if not used[i]: used[i]=True; current.append(i); backtrack(); current.pop(); used[i]=False; backtrack(); return result. Time complexity is O(n*n!) because we generate n! permutations each of length n. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and current list. This feeds into L0 by providing the permutations that become assignments. Common mistakes include not copying the current list before adding to results (leading to references to same list), or not properly backtracking. Real code pattern: def permutations(n): res=[]; path=[]; used=[False]*n; def backtrack(): if len(path)==n: res.append(path[:]); return; for i in range(n): if not used[i]: used[i]=True; path.append(i); backtrack(); path.pop(); used[i]=False; backtrack(); return res.

examples.trace: Start: path=[], used=[F,F]. backtrack(): len=0!=2. Loop i=0: used[0]=True, path=[0]. backtrack(): len=1!=2. Loop i=0: used[0]=True skip. i=1: used[1]=True, path=[0,1]. backtrack(): len=2==2, add copy [0,1] to res. Return, pop path becomes [0], used[1]=False. Return, pop path becomes [], used[0]=False. Loop i=1: used[1]=True, path=[1]. backtrack(): len=1!=2. Loop i=0: used[0]=True, path=[1,0]. backtrack(): len=2==2, add [1,0] to res. Return, pop to [1], used[0]=False. Return, pop to [], used[1]=False. Done. res=[[0,1],[1,0]].

Start path=[], used=[F,F,F]. backtrack(): i=0: path=[0], used[0]=T. backtrack(): i=0 skip, i=1: path=[0,1], used[1]=T. backtrack(): i=0,1 skip, i=2: path=[0,1,2], add [0,1,2]. Backtrack: pop to [0,1], used[2]=F. Return, pop to [0], used[1]=F. i=2: path=[0,2], used[2]=T. backtrack(): i=0 skip, i=1: path=[0,2,1], add [0,2,1]. Backtrack pop to [0,2], used[1]=F. Return pop to [0], used[2]=F. Return pop to [], used[0]=F. i=1: path=[1], used[1]=T. backtrack(): i=0: path=[1,0], used[0]=T. backtrack(): i=0 skip, i=1 skip, i=2: path=[1,0,2], add [1,0,2]. Backtrack similarly generates [1,2,0]. Then i=2: path=[2] generates [2,0,1] and [2,1,0]. Final list as output.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking with State Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement backtracking that tracks used elements to avoid repetitions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core backtracking technique with state tracking. The intuition is to systematically explore all possibilities by making a choice, recursing, then undoing the choice (backtracking), while keeping track of which elements have been used to ensure we don't repeat them. This is essential for generating permutations without duplicates. The algorithm: we maintain a current path (list of chosen elements) and a used array (boolean for each element). At each step, we try all elements that are not yet used, add one to the path, mark it used, recurse to extend further, then upon return, remove it from path and mark unused to try other possibilities. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty path and used array. 2. Define recursive function: if path length equals target length, process complete path. 3. Otherwise, loop through all elements: if element not used, mark used, add to path, recurse, then unmark and remove. Time complexity depends on number of recursive calls; for permutations it's O(n!). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and used array. This connects to L1 as the mechanism to generate permutations. Common mistakes include forgetting to unmark used elements (causing incomplete exploration), or not handling base case correctly. Real code pattern: def backtrack(path, used): if len(path)==target: process(path); return; for i in range(n): if not used[i]: used[i]=True; path.append(i); backtrack(path, used); path.pop(); used[i]=False.

examples.trace: Start: path=[], used=[F,F]. backtrack(): len=0!=2. Loop i=0: not used, used[0]=T, path=[0]. backtrack(): len=1!=2. Loop i=0: used skip. i=1: not used, used[1]=T, path=[0,1]. backtrack(): len=2==2, process [0,1]. Return, path.pop to [0], used[1]=F. Return, path.pop to [], used[0]=F. Loop i=1: not used, used[1]=T, path=[1]. backtrack(): i=0: not used, used[0]=T, path=[1,0]. backtrack(): len=2==2, process [1,0]. Return, pop to [1], used[0]=F. Return, pop to [], used[1]=F. Done.

examples.input: n=3, target length=2 (generate permutations of length 2 from 3 elements)
examples.trace: Start path=[], used=[F,F,F]. backtrack(): len=0!=2. i=0: used[0]=T, path=[0]. backtrack(): len=1!=2. i=0 skip, i=1: used[1]=T, path=[0,1], process. Return, used[1]=F, path=[0]. i=2: used[2]=T, path=[0,2], process. Return, used[2]=F, path=[0]. Return, used[0]=F, path=[]. i=1: used[1]=T, path=[1]. backtrack(): i=0: used[0]=T, path=[1,0], process. i=2: used[2]=T, path=[1,2], process. Similarly for i=2 start. Total 6 paths.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal for Combinatorial Search
ladders.desc: Use recursion to explore all combinations of choices in a decision tree.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces recursive tree traversal for combinatorial search, which is fundamental to backtracking. The intuition is to model the problem as a decision tree where each node represents a partial solution, and we need to explore all paths to leaves (complete solutions). Recursion naturally fits this: at each step, we make a choice, then recursively explore all subsequent choices, and combine results. For assignment, each level of recursion assigns a task to one worker. The algorithm: function explore(level): if level == n: process complete assignment; return; for each possible task for this worker: make choice; explore(level+1); undo choice. This builds a tree with n levels and branching factor decreasing. Time complexity is product of branching factors, for permutations it's n!. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L2 by adding state tracking to avoid reusing tasks. Common mistakes include infinite recursion (missing base case), or not undoing choices (affecting other paths). Real code pattern: def solve(worker): if worker == n: calculate cost; return; for task in range(n): if task not used: assign task to worker; solve(worker+1); unassign task.

examples.trace: Root: worker=0. Level 0: try task=0: assign to worker0, recurse to worker=1. Level 1: try task=1: assign to worker1, recurse to worker=2 (base case), process assignment (0,1). Backtrack, unassign task1, try no other task. Backtrack to level0, unassign task0. Try task=1: assign to worker0, recurse to worker=1. Level1: try task=0: assign, base case process (1,0). Two leaves.

examples.output: Partial tree: from root, branches for task 0,1,2
examples.trace: Root worker=0. Branch task=0: go to worker=1. At worker=1: branch task=1: go to worker=2, then task=2 gives leaf (0,1,2). Or task=2: go to worker=2, task=1 gives leaf (0,2,1). Backtrack to worker=0, branch task=1: similar generates leaves (1,0,2) and (1,2,0). Branch task=2: generates (2,0,1) and (2,1,0). Total 6 leaves.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through All Elements with Condition Check
ladders.desc: Loop through a set of elements and select those that meet a condition (not used).
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation in backtracking: at each step, we need to iterate through all possible choices (e.g., tasks) and select only those that are valid according to current state (e.g., not already assigned). The intuition is simple: we have a list of n elements (tasks 0 to n-1), and a boolean array 'used' that tracks which are already taken. We loop from 0 to n-1, check if used[i] is false, and if so, we can choose that element. This ensures we don't reuse elements, which is necessary for permutations. Algorithm: for i in range(n): if not used[i]: # process element i. Time complexity for one iteration is O(n), but in backtracking this runs many times. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop. This connects to L3 as the action at each recursive node to generate children. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop range, or not correctly checking the condition. Real code pattern: for task in range(n): if not used[task]: # do something with task.

examples.trace: Loop i=0: used[0] is True, so skip. i=1: used[1] is False, so task 1 is valid. i=2: used[2] is False, so task 2 is valid. No more i. Valid tasks are 1 and 2.
i=0: used[0]=False, valid. i=1: used[1]=True, skip. i=2: used[2]=False, valid. i=3: used[3]=True, skip. Valid tasks: 0 and 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Permutations and Factorial Growth
ladders.desc: Learn what permutations are and why n! grows extremely fast.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: a permutation is an arrangement of objects in a specific order. For n distinct items, there are n! (n factorial) permutations, where n! = n × (n-1) × ... × 1. For example, 3! = 6 permutations: (0,1,2), (0,2,1), (1,0,2), (1,2,0), (2,0,1), (2,1,0). The intuition is that for the first position, we have n choices, for the second n-1 choices (since one is already used), and so on, leading to n! total. This exponential growth makes brute force with permutations impractical for large n (e.g., 10! = 3,628,800, 20! ≈ 2.4e18). In the assignment problem, each permutation corresponds to one way to assign tasks to workers. Understanding this helps see why the brute force approach has O(n!) time complexity. Connection to L4: when we iterate to select unused tasks, we are effectively building permutations step by step. Common mistakes include confusing permutations with combinations (where order doesn't matter), or underestimating how fast factorial grows. Real code pattern: To compute factorial: def factorial(n): if n<=1: return 1; return n*factorial(n-1).

examples.trace: List all permutations: Start with 0: then positions left: 1 and 2 can be arranged as 12 or 21, giving 012 and 021. Start with 1: arrangements of 0 and 2: 102 and 120. Start with 2: arrangements of 0 and 1: 201 and 210. Total 6.

Number of permutations = 4×3×2×1 = 24. For example, starting with 0: there are 3! = 6 permutations of remaining 1,2,3: 0123, 0132, 0213, 0231, 0312, 0321. Similarly for starts 1,2,3 each give 6, total 24.

approaches.name: Hungarian Algorithm O(n³)
approaches.summary: Solve the assignment problem directly using the Hungarian algorithm, which is a specialized combinatorial optimization method for finding minimum-cost perfect matching in bipartite graphs. This approach is efficient for standard assignment problems without additional constraints.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Hungarian Algorithm for Assignment Problem
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Hungarian algorithm to find minimum-cost perfect matching in bipartite graphs.
ladders.explanation: The Hungarian algorithm solves the assignment problem by finding a minimum-cost perfect matching in a complete bipartite graph with n workers and n jobs. The intuition is to maintain a feasible labeling (dual variables) while finding augmenting paths to increase matching size. The algorithm works by: 1) Subtract row minima and column minima to get reduced cost matrix. 2) Find maximum matching using zeros in the matrix. 3) If matching is perfect, we're done. 4) Otherwise, adjust labels to create new zeros and repeat. The key insight is that subtracting constants from rows/columns preserves optimal assignments while creating zeros that represent potential assignments. The algorithm terminates when we find n independent zeros (one in each row and column). Time complexity is O(n³) due to up to n augmentations, each taking O(n²). Space is O(n²) for the cost matrix and auxiliary arrays. This connects to L1 by using the augmenting path search as the core subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to update dual variables correctly, improper handling of uncovered zeros, and incorrect termination conditions. Real code pattern involves maintaining arrays for row/column labels, slack values, and predecessor tracking.

examples.trace: Step 1: Subtract row minima: row0-3→[0,2,4], row1-2→[0,2,4], row2-1→[0,2,4]. Step 2: Subtract column minima: col0 already 0, col1-2→[0,0,2], col2-4→[0,0,0]. Step 3: Find maximum matching: row0-col0, row1-col1, row2-col2 gives perfect matching. Step 4: Total cost = original[0][0]+original[1][1]+original[2][2]=3+4+5=12.

Step 1: Row minima: 2,3,1 → subtract: [[7,0,5],[3,1,0],[4,7,0]]. Step 2: Column minima: 3,0,0 → subtract: [[4,0,5],[0,1,0],[1,7,0]]. Step 3: Initial matching: row1-col0, row2-col2. Step 4: Adjust labels: uncovered zeros at (0,1). Step 5: Augment path: (0,1)-(1,1)-(1,0)-(2,0)-(2,2). Step 6: New matching: (0,1), (1,0), (2,2). Step 7: Check perfection: yes. Step 8: Cost = 2+6+1=9? Wait recalc: Actually (0,1)=2, (1,0)=6, (2,2)=1 total=9. But optimal is 10? Let me trace properly: After step 2 matrix [[4,0,5],[0,1,0],[1,7,0]]. Matching: row1-col0 (0), row2-col2 (0). Uncovered zero at (0,1). Augment: add (0,1), conflict at row1? Actually row1 has col0, so find alternating path: (0,1) new, (1,1) not zero, (1,0) zero, (2,0) not zero, (2,2) zero. Flip: remove (1,0), add (0,1) and (2,2) and (1,? Actually need proper augment: Start with free row0, col1 zero, col1 matched to none? Let's do properly: Initial matching M={(1,0),(2,2)}. Find augmenting path from free vertex row0: row0-col1 (zero) free column → augment: M={(0,1),(1,0),(2,2)} perfect. Cost from original: 2+6+1=9. But is 9 minimal? Check other assignments: (0,1)=2, (1,2)=3, (2,0)=5 total=10. So 9 is indeed minimal. My earlier 10 was wrong.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Augmenting Path Search with Slack Updates
ladders.desc: Find augmenting path in equality graph using modified BFS with dual variable adjustments.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine finds an augmenting path in the equality graph (edges with zero reduced cost) to increase matching size. The intuition is to maintain a feasible labeling while searching for alternating paths from unmatched rows to unmatched columns. We use a BFS-like approach that keeps track of slack values for each column (minimum delta to make an edge tight). The algorithm: 1) Initialize sets S (visited rows) and T (visited columns). 2) For each free row, add to S. 3) While there are rows in S not connected to free columns: a) Find minimum slack among edges from S to not-T columns. b) Update labels: subtract slack from S rows, add to T columns. c) Add new tight edges (slack=0) to equality graph. d) If found free column, augment path. Time O(n²) per search. Space O(n). This feeds into L0 by being the core loop that finds perfect matching. Common mistakes: incorrect slack updates, forgetting to reset visited sets between searches, and improper path reconstruction. Real code uses arrays for slack, slackX (which row achieved min slack), and prev for path tracking.

examples.trace: Step 1: S={1}, T={}. Step 2: From row1, edges: col0=1, col1=0, col2=3. Add col1 to T (zero). Step 3: col1 matched to row0, add row0 to S. Step 4: From S={1,0}, edges to not-T: col0: min(1,0)=0 via row0, col2: min(3,0)=0 via row0. Add col0,col2 to T. Step 5: col2 free, augment path: row1-col1-row0-col0-row2-col2. Flip: remove (0,0), add (1,1),(0,0),(2,2).
Step 1: S={1}, T={}. Step 2: From row1, edges: col0=0, col1=3, col2=1. Add col0 to T (zero). Step 3: col0 free, augment directly: row1-col0. New matching: (0,1),(1,0).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Feasible Labeling and Reduced Cost Matrix
ladders.desc: Maintain dual variables (labels) that satisfy reduced cost non-negativity with zeros representing admissible edges.
ladders.explanation: The core technique is maintaining a feasible labeling: row labels u[i] and column labels v[j] such that for all i,j: cost[i][j] - u[i] - v[j] ≥ 0. The reduced cost matrix is c'[i][j] = cost[i][j] - u[i] - v[j]. Zeros in this matrix represent edges that are 'tight' and form the equality graph. The intuition is that subtracting constants from rows/columns doesn't change the optimal assignment, but creates zeros that guide the matching process. Initially, set u[i] = min(row i) and v[j]=0, then iteratively adjust labels during augmenting path search. When we need to create new zeros, we compute delta = min{c'[i][j] for i in S, j not in T}, then update: u[i] -= delta for i in S, v[j] += delta for j in T. This preserves feasibility while reducing some positive reduced costs to zero. Time O(n²) per update. Space O(n). This connects to L1 by providing the labels used in the augmenting path search. Common mistakes: updating labels incorrectly (sign errors), not maintaining feasibility, and improper delta computation. Real code pattern involves two arrays u and v updated during the search.

examples.output: Reduced matrix [[0,2,4],[0,2,4],[0,2,4]], after column minima v=[0,2,4]
examples.trace: Step 1: u = row minima: row0 min=3, row1=2, row2=1. Step 2: Reduced: c'[0][0]=3-3-0=0, c'[0][1]=5-3-0=2, c'[0][2]=7-3-0=4. Similarly row1: [0,2,4], row2: [0,2,4]. Step 3: Column minima: col0=0, col1=2, col2=4. Update v: v[0]+=0, v[1]+=2, v[2]+=4. Step 4: New reduced: subtract column minima: row0: [0-0,2-2,4-4]=[0,0,0], similarly all rows become [0,0,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Bipartite Matching with Augmenting Paths
ladders.desc: Find maximum matching in bipartite graph using augmenting path algorithm (similar to Hopcroft-Karp but simpler).
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves finding maximum matching in bipartite graphs by repeatedly finding augmenting paths. An augmenting path starts and ends at free vertices, alternating between unmatched and matched edges. Flipping edges along such path increases matching size by 1. The intuition is Berge's lemma: a matching is maximum iff there is no augmenting path. The algorithm: 1) Start with empty matching. 2) While exists augmenting path: a) Find path using BFS/DFS. b) Flip matched/unmatched status along path. 3) Return matching. Time O(VE) with simple DFS, space O(V+E). This connects to L2 by being the underlying graph algorithm used on the equality graph. Common mistakes: not alternating properly, incorrect path reconstruction, and infinite loops. Real code uses adjacency lists and visited arrays for DFS.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start empty. Step 2: Find augmenting path for row0: row0-col0 free, add (0,0). Step 3: For row1: row1-col1 free, add (1,1). Step 4: For row2: row2-col0 but col0 matched to row0, find alternating path: row2-col0-row0-col1 free, flip: remove (0,0), add (2,0),(0,1). Step 5: Now row0 matched, row1 matched, row2 matched. Size 3.
Step 1: Empty. Step 2: Add (0,0). Step 3: Add (1,1). Step 4: Row2: try col0 matched to row0, no augmenting path to free column. Try col1 matched to row1, no path. So maximum size 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Matrix Row and Column Minima Subtraction
ladders.desc: Subtract smallest element from each row and column to create zero entries while preserving optimal assignment.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation transforms the cost matrix by subtracting constants from rows and columns without changing the optimal assignment. The intuition: if we subtract a constant from all entries in a row, every assignment using that row has its cost reduced by that constant, so relative costs between assignments remain same. Similarly for columns. By subtracting row minima and column minima, we create zeros that help identify potential assignments. The algorithm: 1) For each row i, find min_row[i] = min(cost[i][j]). 2) Subtract min_row[i] from all elements in row i. 3) For each column j, find min_col[j] = min(cost[i][j] after row subtraction). 4) Subtract min_col[j] from all elements in column j. Time O(n²), space O(n). This connects to L3 by creating the initial reduced cost matrix for matching. Common mistakes: subtracting from wrong matrix, not updating costs correctly, and misunderstanding that this preserves optimality. Real code uses simple nested loops.

examples.trace: Step 1: Row minima: 3,2,1. Subtract: row0: [0,2,4], row1: [0,2,4], row2: [0,2,4]. Step 2: Column minima: col0=0, col1=2, col2=4. Subtract: col1: subtract 2 from each: row0[1]=0, row1[1]=0, row2[1]=0. col2: subtract 4: all become 0. Result: all zeros.

Step 1: Row minima: 2,3,1. Subtract: row0: [7,0,5], row1: [3,1,0], row2: [4,7,0]. Step 2: Column minima: col0=3, col1=0, col2=0. Subtract col0: row0[0]=4, row1[0]=0, row2[0]=1. Others unchanged. Result: [[4,0,5],[0,1,0],[1,7,0]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Bipartite Graphs and Matchings
ladders.desc: Understand bipartite graphs, matchings, and perfect matchings as prerequisite concepts.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph has vertices divided into two disjoint sets U and V such that every edge connects a vertex in U to one in V. A matching is a set of edges without common vertices. A perfect matching covers all vertices (each vertex has exactly one incident edge in the matching). For assignment problem, U represents workers, V represents jobs, edges have costs, and we want minimum-cost perfect matching. The intuition is that we need to assign each worker to exactly one job and each job to exactly one worker. This is foundational because Hungarian algorithm operates on bipartite graphs. Key properties: bipartite graphs can be colored with two colors, have no odd cycles. Maximum matching size ≤ min(|U|,|V|). Perfect matching requires |U|=|V|. Time to check bipartiteness O(V+E). Space O(V+E). This connects to L4 by providing the graph structure for matrix operations. Common mistakes: confusing bipartite with general graphs, misunderstanding matching constraints. Real code uses adjacency list representation and BFS for bipartite checking.

examples.output: Maximum matching {(0,b),(1,a)} size 2, not perfect since |U|=|V|=2 but possible perfect? Actually perfect exists: (0,a),(1,b) but no edge (1,b). So maximum size 2 is perfect? Wait |U|=2, |V|=2, matching size 2 covers all vertices if edges exist. Here (0,b) and (1,a) covers all, so perfect matching.
examples.trace: Step 1: Graph is bipartite: U and V separate. Step 2: Find matching: start with (0,a). Step 3: Add (1,b) but no edge. Step 4: Alternative: (0,b) and (1,a) works, covers all vertices. So perfect matching exists.
Step 1: Bipartite: all edges between U and V. Step 2: Since |U|=|V|=3 and complete graph, perfect matching always exists. Step 3: Example matching pairs each U vertex with distinct V vertex.

testCases.expected: Maximum matching size 2, not perfect since |U|≠|V|
Perfect matching exists: {(0,a),(1,b),(2,c)} but no edge (2,c)? Actually no edge (2,c), so maximum size 2, not perfect
